A2-J .2 The StripID shall follow the SEMI T9 Specification, when applicable, or may be user definable by the factory. A2-l.3 For any given process there should be a way to ensure correct orientation of the strip. The end user is responsible for providing a master manufacturing drawing showing the top side of the strip. Thjs drawing should show any special markings or patterns that are needed to reliably flip and rotate a physical strip until it is oriented the same as the drawing. Figure A2-l defines what is meant by top side and hence bottom side. It also defines what is meant by upper, lower and left and right. A2-l .4 Each strip type should have a factory defined value for OriginLocation. Thjs is the origin reference for the row 1, column I location on the strip. The factory OriginLocation is selected as one of four comers by the factory host system. The row and column index should be referenced from the selected reference. This information should be provided to the equipment by the host in each strip map download scenario. 1 = Upper right (UR) top side 2 = Upper left (UL) top side 3 = Lower left (LL) top side 4 = Lower right (LR) top side 6 = Upper right (UR) bottom side 7 = Upper left (UL) bottom side 8 = Lower left (LL) bottom

It is modelled as a substrate with SubstrateType=TnR. Each pocket on the tape has an XY coordinate just like any other substrate. These pockets may have the pocket number marked on the tape which can be read by the equipment placing devices on, or picking them from, the tape. In this case, FX, FY, TX, TY for the Transfer can be recorded as indicated on the pocket. R2-3.2.2 In many cases, however, the tape and reel does not have pocket numbers but instead a unique pocket identifier (PI). Unit's most basic representation is shown in Figure I. The attribute definitions are described below. Unit Symbol Name Description Figure 1 Unit Object Definition 6.2.l Symbol - A unit symbol is a unique text string of unspecified length that represents the unit's measurement value and adds physical significance to the numeric value it represents. This unique identifier is in many cases all that is needed to describe a value and add some significance to it. The symbol is case sensitive. 6.2.2 Name - String containing the full name of the unit of measurement. 6.2.3 Description - Information that describes the unit of measurement. In some cases, description provides a definition of the unit given by an industry or standards development organization. 7 Basis of Classification 7. I In general, units are grouped into some special category. These categories are chosen to describe the units' natural classification. In this Standard, the symbol that represents the measurement unit classifies the unit under one of three categories: Basic, Modified, and Combined. 7.2 Unit Symbol Categories 7.2.1 Basic Unit-Units

NOTE 34: As stated above, the number of occurrences of harm is to be considered in determining the Likelihood for each observed or foreseen harm. This is not necessarily the same as the number of occurrences of the observed or foreseen event. For example, if a single leak occurs that results in two people requiring medical treatment, but recovering fully, that is counted as two occurrences of the harm. Put another way, the Likelihood and, therefore the Risk, depend on the number of people harmed, but the Severity does not. R2-3.6 Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) - A tabulation of the system/plant equipment, failure modes, and each failure mode's effect on the system/plant equipment. The failure mode is a description of how equipment fails (e.g., open, closed, on, off, leaks). The effect of the failure mode is the system response or accident resulting from the equipment failure. FMEA identifies single failure modes that either directly result in or contribute significantly to an important accident. Human/operator errors are generally not examined in FMEA, although the effects of a misoperation are usually described by an equipment failure mode. FMEA is not efficient for identifying combinations of equipment failures that lead to accidents. The FMEA can be performed by two analysts or a multi-disciplinary team of professionals. A Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) is an FMEA with criticality rankings (based on MIL-STD-l629A). R2-3. 7 What-if - Possible failures are checked on possible consequences. R2-3.8 Event Tree Analysis - Event tree analysis is a technique for evaluating potential accident outcomes resulting from a specific equipment failure or human error known as an initiating event. Event tree analysis considers operator response or safety system response to the initiating event in determining the potential accident outcomes. The results of the event tree analysis are accident sequences, a chronological set of failures or errors that define an accident. These results describe the possible accident outcomes in terms of the sequence of events (successes or failures of safety functions) that follow an initiating event. Event tree analysis is well suited for operations that have safety systems or emergency procedures in place to respond to specific initiating events. SEMI S10-0423E SEMI 1996, 2023 semr NOTE 53: This technique is also known as Fault Hazard Analysis. R2-3.9 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) - FTA focuses on one particular accident event and provides a method for determining causes of that accident event. The fault tree itself is a graphic model that displays the various combinations of equipment faults and failures that can result in the accident event. The solution of the fault tree is a list of the sets of equipment failures that are sufficient to result in the accident event of interest. The strength of FT A as a qualitative tool is its ability to break down an accident into basic failures.

So this likelihood is estimated to be A - Frequent. R5-3.4.2.2 Sensor Malfunctions - The sensor is of a reliable type that is guaranteed for 500,000 cycles. Based on the number of times the product is placed on the equipment and that the sensor has to cycle once for each time the product is placed (present and not present) x IO times/hour x 24 hours x 365 days = 87,600 times a year. If the sensor were to fail at its guaranteed lifetime, it would fail once every 5.7 years = 0.175 times per year. For the purposes of this example, we assume that all sensor failures result in the hatch closing. But the only time the error has effect for the operator is if the failure occurs during manually placing the product. Manual placement frequency is: 2 manual placements day 2 manual placements (10 automatic placements day + hour x 24 hours) x 100% = 0.83% day of all placements. So the expected frequency of operator entrapment because of sensor malfunction is: (RS-I) 0.175 sensor failures per year x 0.83% manual placement = 0.00145 = 0.145% = 1.45

I Accidentally Lowering the Cassette - The frequency with which the operator makes a wrong movement with the product cassette will not change. To reduce the probability of entrapment when the operator does this, the time before closing can be set to 5 seconds, the speed of closing reduced, and a warning signal can be given to the operator so that there is sufficient time to move out of the hazard zone. In this case, we estimate the Likelihood of entrapment if the operator has warning and extra time to move out of the danger zone as D - Rare or E - Unlikely. This would reduce the risk of entrapment of the operator because of accidentally lifting or lowering the cassette to Low or Very Low. R5-3.5.2 Sensor Malfunction - A sensor with higher guaranteed lifetime (2 x I 06 cycles, 4 times the present sensor's lifetime) can be selected or a second sensor can be installed. By using the higher reliability component the expected frequency of entrapment because of sensor failure will be a factor of 4 lower: 3.625 x I Q-4 entrapments per year. The Likelihood would therefore be D - Rare. By using a second sensor with logic that compares both sensors the expected frequency is more difficult to calculate (failure of both components of the same time or failure of one sensor and the logic) but such a change is expected to reduce the expected failure frequency well below 2 x 10-5 per unit year. The Likelihood would be rated: Not reasonably foreseeable. This would reduce the risk of entrapment because of sensor failure to Very Low.
